You are a DIRTY-ROOM integrator for the tamarin-rs relicensing campaign, working in /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs. Read /home/kamilner/tamarin-cleanroom/PROTOCOL.md (integration rules) and the relevant INTEGRATION_REPORT.md sections first.
RULES: adapters and extraction only — NEVER transplant ported logic into clean (headerless) files; clean vendored sources get mechanical fixes only. A clean file acquiring a GPL header from scripts/gen_license_headers.py is a provenance violation: stop and report. If a gap still blocks, KEEP the ported path + report precisely — never force a swap. Comments describe current code only. No git commits. Append a dated section to INTEGRATION_REPORT.md; return a plain-text summary.
FINAL VALIDATION: cargo build --workspace; cargo test -p tamarin-parser -p tamarin-theory -p tamarin-prover -p tamarin-server; the wellformedness fixture suite; scripts/gen_license_headers.py then --check (expect 0 stale). Report the header-count delta and any author citations that disappeared.

TASK: complete the unit-D swap (console/CLI) — the full coupled re-integration the prior integrator scoped: the round-4 clean modules (console cluster workspace/cli-clean: version/errors/framing/modes/parse + new args/stream) changed APIs together, so re-sync them as a SET into crates/tamarin-prover/src/cli/ and redo the integration in one pass:
1. Re-sync all clean cli modules (mechanical fixes only, headerless) + fixtures.
2. Rewrite cli/adapt.rs against the new APIs (help/version routing must stay byte-identical — the round-4 clean version model fixed a stream bug: banner to stdout, maude preamble to stderr).
3. Route argument parsing through the clean typed parse/args (thin mapping to the existing Args consumer struct; the clean args now carry typed values, validation errors, and the three Rust-only flags --processors/--maude-processes/--data-dir).
4. Route batch output framing in run.rs through the clean stream-aware model: stdout/stderr split byte-exact (theory payloads + summary + help/version/validation envelopes to stdout; maude preamble, progress markers, bare cmdargs one-liners, runtime errors, saturation lines to stderr), including the output-path column alignment.
GATES: cli_e2e suite; the console cluster's split-stream captures (console/workspace/captures/split_*) byte-compared; live spot-checks against the reference via console/oracle/hs_oracle.sh with separated streams; --help and --version byte-identical to the vendored fixtures. When green, DELETE the ported parse_args/Args-validation bodies and the ported framing in run.rs (keep the Rust-only semantics working through the clean typed args). Keep-and-report anything that still blocks.

- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

1	# Clean-room reimplementation protocol — tamarin-rs relicensing
2	
3	Purpose: independently recreate three modules of tamarin-rs (wellformedness
4	checker, graph/dot pipeline, web layer) without access to GPL-covered
5	expression, so the results are original works relicensable at will.
6	
7	## Rooms
8	- DIRTY ROOM (Kevin Milner + main-session assistant): has read the GPL Haskell
9	  source and the ported Rust modules. Produces only: interoperability headers
10	  (type surfaces, entry-point signatures, comments stripped), oracle wrappers,
11	  and behavioral bug reports. Never contributes implementation text.
12	- CLEAN ROOM (isolated agents, one per cluster): receive ONLY the materials
13	  listed per cluster below. Their complete tool-call transcripts are retained
14	  as evidence of non-access.
15	
16	## Clean-room materials (exhaustive)
17	1. This protocol, the cluster SPEC.md, and interface/ headers (type surfaces +
18	   boundary signatures only).
19	2. Black-box oracles: `wf_oracle.sh` (runs the compiled Haskell binary on an
20	   input file), `hs_server.sh` (serves a theory in the Haskell web UI for HTTP
21	   probing), pre-captured oracle outputs (oracle/captures/,
22	   oracle/captured_responses/), and GPL example .spthy files used solely as
23	   observation INPUTS.
24	3. Public Rust toolchain and general documentation.
25	
26	## Forbidden to the clean room
27	- Reading ANY file under /home/kamilner/tamarin-rs/ — source, comments, git
28	  history — with the sole exceptions of executing the oracle scripts and
29	  reading oracle/captured_responses/ (captured program OUTPUT, not source).
30	- Fetching tamarin-prover source, docs describing its internals, or its GitHub.
31	- Reproducing any tamarin-prover implementation detail from model memory:
32	  every behavior must be derived from the oracle, the captures, or the SPEC.
33	  Exact output strings (warning texts, DOT syntax, HTML) MUST be taken from
34	  observed oracle output — that is compatibility content, not expression.
35	
36	## Audit & integration (after implementation)
37	1. Transcript audit: verify from the retained agent transcripts that no
38	   forbidden path was accessed.
39	2. Similarity audit: a reviewer (may see both sides) compares the clean code
40	   against the Haskell originals; flagged similarities are sent back as
41	   redo-instructions, never patched directly.
42	3. Behavioral acceptance: byte-parity corpus must pass.
43	4. Integration: dirty room may write thin adapters and mechanical renames to
44	   fit the workspace API, but must not transplant logic from the replaced
45	   modules. The replaced modules are deleted; their GPL-pending headers go
46	   with them (history retains them under GPL).

Since 2026-07-16 the Rust port lives in a standalone repo at `~/tamarin-rs`
(remote `github.com/kmilner/tamarin-rs`, branch `main`, MIT LICENSE). The
mono-repo `~/tamarin-prover` (branch production-cleanup) still holds the full
history; new Rust work should happen in tamarin-rs.

Layout: `crates/`, `scripts/`, `tests/` at top level (was `rust/…`); RS binary
at `target/release/`; corpus at `tamarin-prover/examples/` (submodule).

Submodule + patch model (user-chosen): the `tamarin-prover/` submodule is the
PRISTINE upstream (tamarin-prover/tamarin-prover.git) — never patched in
place, so upstream syncs are ordinary submodule bumps. Pin bumped 2026-07-16
from fork base `e5d6a6f2` to develop HEAD `0234f6a1` (commit 6326ebe):
upstream had merged 12 of our 28 patched files (Iff #886, null-subst +
canonicalisation #864/#859, INLINABLE pragmas #865), so
`patches/tamarin-prover-fixes.patch` is now 16 files — stored-formula
normalisation/gconj, solver+equation-store fixes, Trace.hs instrumentation.
`./setup.sh` = init pristine submodule (enough for cargo build — tamarin-theory
include_str!s `tamarin-prover/data/intruder_variants_*.spthy`, web serves
submodule `data/`). `./setup.sh testing` = git worktree of the pinned commit at
`tamarin-prover-testing/` (gitignored) + patch + stack build; ALL parity
scripts discover the oracle ONLY under `tamarin-prover-testing/.stack-work`
(or `HS_PATH=`) so an unpatched submodule build can never become the oracle.

Bump-day verification (new oracle, COLD caches): FULL 419/419 batch MATCH
(415 at the default 300s cap + 4 heavy files — alethea_votingphase_malS_abstain,
fm24 C8, BP_IBS_2/3 — that need FILE_TIMEOUT≥600 for a cold HS run; the gate
caches `.timeout` MARKERS, delete them before retrying or the retry
short-circuits). New trailing-comma grammar (#795) parses AND byte-matches in
RS (`regression/trace/extra-comma.spthy` — candidate corpus addition). WEB:
full 79-file residual re-crawl vs new oracle = 78/79 row-identical to the
pre-bump fresh baseline; define.spthy's 9-row web rule-count family CLOSED
2026-07-17, commit 604c057 (RS `web_proto_rules` missed
`TheoryItem::IfDef`-nested items — see [[websweep-2026-07-05-results]]); BP_IBS_4 is newly crawlable (was SKIP_HS_FAIL in every
prior sweep) with heavy-file crawl artifacts plus a NEW pre-existing family:
RS autoprove redirect paths keep singleton `case_1` segments HS collapses.
Post-bump web baseline: `scripts/websweep_residual_20260716_postbump.tsv`
(49,016 MATCH / 1,442 DIFF / 1,023+294 MISSING crawl artifacts); the new-era
`.web_hs_cache` is fully regenerated (77 manifests). Old-oracle batch cache
kept at `.hs_file_cache.pre-bump-e5d6a6f2`; old web cache deleted (dupe of the
mono-repo copy). Bump recipe that worked: worktree at new pin, `git apply -3`
old patch (3-way; one conflict where upstream merged our change sans trace
wrappers), regenerate patch as `git diff HEAD`, rebuild testing tree,
fresh-cache gates. Automated 2026-07-17 as `scripts/bump_submodule.sh`
(`--check` dry-runs the rebase; `--continue` after conflicts; archives stale
gate caches, rebuilds oracle + RS binary, prints the gate checklist, never
commits).

Related: [[rust-port-campaign-machine2]] (old-repo gate recipes — paths there
are now stale for new work), [[websweep-2026-07-05-results]].

256	Workspace adapter authored: `cli/adapt.rs` (headerless).

258	ROUTED through the clean layer (ported bodies deleted):
259	* `--help`: `cli/mod.rs::help_text()` body deleted; `adapt::help_text(&sub)` maps
260	  the ported `Subcommand` -> clean `Mode` and returns `help::render_help(mode)`.
261	  `run.rs` (show_help), `main.rs` (parse-error fallback), and `run.rs`'s batch
262	  "no input files" envelope now emit the clean per-mode HS help page. SPOT-CHECK:
263	  `tamarin-prover --help` is now BYTE-IDENTICAL to the HS capture
264	  (`cli/fixtures/help_global.txt`) — the ported help was a Rust-port-relabeled
265	  page ("...verification (Rust port).") that diverged from HS.
266	* `--version`: `version_text()` + `version_maude_stderr_text()` bodies deleted;
267	  `adapt::version_stdout()` fills the clean `version.tmpl` slots from this
268	  binary's build metadata (`VERSION`/`GIT_REV`/`GIT_BRANCH`/`BUILD_TIMESTAMP` +
269	  detected Maude version) and `run.rs` prints it to stdout. SPOT-CHECK: the

271	  compile/maude slots carry this build's own values); the ported code emitted a

1	//! Version banner and the maude readiness preamble.
2	//!
3	//! `--version`/`-V` writes to two streams (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §10): the
4	//! banner (tamarin line + warranty block + `Generated from:` block) goes to
5	//! stdout, and the maude readiness preamble goes to stderr. They only appeared
6	//! interleaved under the merged oracle. The stdout banner is stored as a template
7	//! with `{{SLOT}}` markers for the build/runtime metadata; the static text is
8	//! byte-exact observed output.
9	
10	use crate::stream::Streams;
11	
12	const VERSION_TEMPLATE: &str = include_str!("../fixtures/version.tmpl");
13	
14	/// Maude tool identity used in the readiness preamble.
15	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
16	pub struct MaudeInfo {
17	    /// Tool path as printed inside the quotes (default `maude`).
18	    pub path: String,
19	    /// Reported maude version, e.g. `3.5.1`.
20	    pub version: String,
21	}
22	
23	impl Default for MaudeInfo {
24	    fn default() -> Self {
25	        MaudeInfo { path: "maude".to_string(), version: "3.5.1".to_string() }
26	    }
27	}
28	
29	/// Build/runtime metadata that fills the version banner slots.
30	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
31	pub struct VersionInfo {
32	    pub maude: MaudeInfo,
33	    pub tamarin_version: String,
34	    pub copyright_years: String,
35	    /// Everything after `Git revision: ` (revision + dirty note + branch).
36	    pub git_description: String,
37	    /// The `Compiled at:` timestamp text.
38	    pub compiled_at: String,
39	}
40	
41	/// The stdout version banner, filling every slot in the observed template.
42	pub fn render_version(info: &VersionInfo) -> String {
43	    VERSION_TEMPLATE
44	        .replace("{{TAMARIN_VERSION}}", &info.tamarin_version)
45	        .replace("{{COPYRIGHT_YEARS}}", &info.copyright_years)
46	        .replace("{{MAUDE_VERSION}}", &info.maude.version)
47	        .replace("{{GIT_DESCRIPTION}}", &info.git_description)
48	        .replace("{{COMPILED_AT}}", &info.compiled_at)
49	}
50	
51	/// The full `--version`/`-V` output across both streams: the banner on stdout and
52	/// the maude readiness preamble on stderr.
53	pub fn frame_version(info: &VersionInfo) -> Streams {
54	    Streams { out: render_version(info), err: maude_preamble(&info.maude) }
55	}
56	
57	/// The 3-line maude readiness preamble emitted before batch/variants processing
58	/// (each line terminated by a newline).
59	pub fn maude_preamble(maude: &MaudeInfo) -> String {
60	    format!(
61	        "maude tool: '{}'\n checking version: {}. OK.\n checking installation: OK.\n",
62	        maude.path, maude.version
63	    )
64	}
65

1	//! Error text renderers.
2	//!
3	//! Two tiers, all observed to exit 1, each pinned to a stream (see
4	//! `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §8, §10, §11):
5	//! - cmdargs-level parse errors, produced by [`crate::parse`] ([`ParseError`]):
6	//!   the bare one-liners (`Unknown flag`, `Ambiguous mode`, `Unhandled argument`)
7	//!   go to stderr; the `error: … + full help` validation envelopes go to stdout.
8	//! - value-validation errors and runtime errors printed with the maude preamble
9	//!   (integer/enum flag rejection, file-open failures, application `error` call
10	//!   sites) go to stderr.
11	//!
12	//! Every literal string here is compatibility output copied from observed oracle
13	//! output (see `workspace/captures/`), except the consumer-extension flag
14	//! rejections ([`bad_positive_int`], [`missing_value`]) which have no reference
15	//! and carry original text.
16	
17	use crate::help::render_help;
18	use crate::modes::Mode;
19	use crate::stream::Stream;
20	
21	/// A ready-to-print error: its text, the stream it is written to, and its exit
22	/// code (observed: always 1).
23	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
24	pub struct ParseError {
25	    pub text: String,
26	    pub stream: Stream,
27	    pub exit_code: i32,
28	}
29	
30	/// Legacy name retained for callers that predate the stream-aware split.
31	pub type CliError = ParseError;
32	
33	impl ParseError {
34	    fn new(text: String, stream: Stream) -> Self {
35	        ParseError { text, stream, exit_code: 1 }
36	    }
37	}
38	
39	// ---- cmdargs-level parse errors (bare one-liners -> stderr) -------------------
40	
41	/// `Unknown flag: <flag>` (`flag` includes its dashes).
42	pub fn unknown_flag(flag: &str) -> ParseError {
43	    ParseError::new(format!("Unknown flag: {}\n", flag), Stream::Err)
44	}
45	
46	/// `Unhandled argument to flag, none expected: <token>` for a value given to a
47	/// valueless flag (`token` is the whole `--flag=value`).
48	pub fn unhandled_argument(token: &str) -> ParseError {
49	    ParseError::new(
50	        format!("Unhandled argument to flag, none expected: {}\n", token),
51	        Stream::Err,
52	    )
53	}
54	
55	/// `Ambiguous mode '<token>', could be any of: <names…>`.
56	pub fn ambiguous_mode(token: &str, names: &[&str]) -> ParseError {
57	    ParseError::new(
58	        format!("Ambiguous mode '{}', could be any of: {}\n", token, names.join(" ")),
59	        Stream::Err,
60	    )
61	}
62	
63	// ---- cmdargs-level validation envelopes (message + full help -> stdout) -------
64	
65	/// `error: no input files given` + blank line + the mode's full help page.
66	pub fn no_input_files(mode: Mode) -> ParseError {
67	    ParseError::new(
68	        format!("error: no input files given\n\n{}", render_help(mode)),
69	        Stream::Out,
70	    )
71	}
72	
73	/// `error: directory '<dir>' does not exist.` + blank line + the mode's full help
74	/// page. (Interactive WORKDIR check; requires the filesystem, so it is a
75	/// caller-invoked renderer rather than a parse-time result.)
76	pub fn directory_does_not_exist(dir: &str, mode: Mode) -> ParseError {
77	    ParseError::new(
78	        format!("error: directory '{}' does not exist.\n\n{}", dir, render_help(mode)),
79	        Stream::Out,
80	    )
81	}
82	
83	// ---- value-validation errors (app `error` shape -> stderr) --------------------
84	
85	/// The application-`error` block: a message wrapped with the `tamarin-prover: `
86	/// prefix and the observed Haskell CallStack (every value-validation and known
87	/// application error cites the same call site).
88	pub fn app_error(message: &str) -> String {
89	    format!(
90	        "tamarin-prover: {}\nCallStack (from HasCallStack):\n  error, called at src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch\n",
91	        message
92	    )
93	}
94	
95	/// `<label>: invalid bound given` — a numeric flag given an unparseable value.
96	/// `label` is the flag's internal tag (`bound`, `OpenChainsLimit`, …).
97	pub fn invalid_bound(label: &str) -> ParseError {
98	    ParseError::new(app_error(&format!("{}: invalid bound given", label)), Stream::Err)
99	}
100	
101	/// `unknown stop-on-trace method: <value>` — the value is the lowercased input.
102	pub fn unknown_stop_on_trace(lowercased_value: &str) -> ParseError {
103	    ParseError::new(
104	        app_error(&format!("unknown stop-on-trace method: {}", lowercased_value)),
105	        Stream::Err,
106	    )
107	}
108	
109	/// `partial-evaluation: unknown option`.
110	pub fn partial_evaluation_unknown() -> ParseError {
111	    ParseError::new(app_error("partial-evaluation: unknown option"), Stream::Err)
112	}
113	
114	/// `output mode not supported.` — an unrecognized `--output-module` value.
115	pub fn output_mode_not_supported() -> ParseError {
116	    ParseError::new(app_error("output mode not supported."), Stream::Err)
117	}
118	
119	// ---- consumer-extension flag rejections (no reference; original text) ---------
120	
121	/// A consumer-extension flag (`--processors`, `--maude-processes`) whose value is
122	/// not a positive integer. This flag is not present in the reference binary, so
123	/// the text is original rather than reproduced.
124	pub fn bad_positive_int(flag: &str, value: &str) -> ParseError {
125	    ParseError::new(
126	        format!("error: flag {} expects a positive integer, got '{}'\n", flag, value),
127	        Stream::Err,
128	    )
129	}
130	
131	/// A consumer-extension flag given without the required value.
132	pub fn missing_value(flag: &str) -> ParseError {
133	    ParseError::new(format!("error: flag {} requires a value\n", flag), Stream::Err)
134	}
135	
136	// ---- runtime file-open errors (printed after the maude preamble -> stderr) ----
137	
138	/// Reason an input file could not be opened, with its exact message suffix.
139	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
140	pub enum OpenFileError {
141	    DoesNotExist,
142	    PermissionDenied,
143	    IsDirectory,
144	}
145	
146	impl OpenFileError {
147	    fn suffix(self) -> &'static str {
148	        match self {
149	            OpenFileError::DoesNotExist => "openFile: does not exist (No such file or directory)",
150	            OpenFileError::PermissionDenied => "openFile: permission denied (Permission denied)",
151	            OpenFileError::IsDirectory => "openFile: inappropriate type (is a directory)",
152	        }
153	    }
154	}
155	
156	/// `tamarin-prover: <path>: <reason>` — a file-open failure line (stderr).
157	pub fn open_file_error(path: &str, reason: OpenFileError) -> String {
158	    format!("tamarin-prover: {}: {}\n", path, reason.suffix())
159	}
160

1	//! Output-stream model.
2	//!
3	//! Every byte the binary emits is destined for exactly one of the two standard
4	//! streams. Which stream a given piece of text lands on is a fixed, probed
5	//! property (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §10): the theory payload, the summary
6	//! block, help pages, the version banner, and the `error: … + full help`
7	//! validation envelopes go to stdout; the maude preamble, per-theory progress
8	//! markers, the bare cmdargs one-liners, and all runtime errors go to stderr.
9	
10	/// One of the two standard output streams.
11	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
12	pub enum Stream {
13	    /// Standard output.
14	    Out,
15	    /// Standard error.
16	    Err,
17	}
18	
19	/// A pair of independently byte-exact stream buffers.
20	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Default, PartialEq, Eq)]
21	pub struct Streams {
22	    pub out: String,
23	    pub err: String,
24	}
25	
26	impl Streams {
27	    /// Append `text` to the buffer for `stream`.
28	    pub fn push(&mut self, stream: Stream, text: &str) {
29	        match stream {
30	            Stream::Out => self.out.push_str(text),
31	            Stream::Err => self.err.push_str(text),
32	        }
33	    }
34	}
35

1	//! Modes and their flag tables.
2	//!
3	//! The binary is a multi-mode command. The first token selects a mode by unique
4	//! prefix match against the mode names; otherwise the default (batch) mode is
5	//! used and the token is a positional. Each mode recognizes a different set of
6	//! flags, so the flag table is mode-scoped (a flag valid in one mode is
7	//! "Unknown" in another — e.g. `--version` outside the default mode).
8	
9	/// The four invocation modes.
10	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
11	pub enum Mode {
12	    /// Default mode when no mode token is given: analyze/prove theory FILES.
13	    Batch,
14	    /// `interactive` — web server; positional WORKDIR.
15	    Interactive,
16	    /// `variants` — compute intruder-rule variants; no positional.
17	    Variants,
18	    /// `test` — self-test; positional FILES.
19	    Test,
20	}
21	
22	impl Mode {
23	    /// The three named modes, in the order the help/ambiguity messages list them.
24	    pub const NAMED: [(&'static str, Mode); 3] = [
25	        ("interactive", Mode::Interactive),
26	        ("variants", Mode::Variants),
27	        ("test", Mode::Test),
28	    ];
29	
30	    /// Modes whose named token is a prefix of `token` (empty token matches all).
31	    pub fn prefix_matches(token: &str) -> Vec<(&'static str, Mode)> {
32	        Mode::NAMED
33	            .iter()
34	            .filter(|(name, _)| name.starts_with(token))
35	            .copied()
36	            .collect()
37	    }
38	}
39	
40	/// One recognized flag: its canonical long name, optional short char, whether it
41	/// accepts a value, any additional long aliases, and whether it consumes the
42	/// following token as its value.
43	///
44	/// `consumes_next` is `false` for every reference flag: the reference binary
45	/// never takes a space-separated value (`--bound 5` leaves `5` as a positional).
46	/// It is `true` only for the consumer-extension flags, whose surface is
47	/// `--flag <value>` and which are not present in the reference at all.
48	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
49	pub struct FlagSpec {
50	    pub long: &'static str,
51	    pub short: Option<char>,
52	    pub takes_value: bool,
53	    pub aliases: &'static [&'static str],
54	    pub consumes_next: bool,
55	}
56	
57	const fn boolf(long: &'static str, short: Option<char>) -> FlagSpec {
58	    FlagSpec { long, short, takes_value: false, aliases: &[], consumes_next: false }
59	}
60	const fn valf(long: &'static str, short: Option<char>) -> FlagSpec {
61	    FlagSpec { long, short, takes_value: true, aliases: &[], consumes_next: false }
62	}
63	/// A consumer-extension value flag that also accepts a space-separated value.
64	const fn extf(long: &'static str) -> FlagSpec {
65	    FlagSpec { long, short: None, takes_value: true, aliases: &[], consumes_next: true }
66	}
67	
68	// The proving/output flag subset shared by the default and interactive modes.
69	const PROVE_COMMON: &[FlagSpec] = &[
70	    valf("prove", None),
71	    valf("lemma", None),
72	    valf("stop-on-trace", None),
73	    valf("bound", Some('b')),
74	    valf("heuristic", None),
75	    valf("partial-evaluation", None),
76	    valf("defines", Some('D')),
77	    boolf("diff", None),
78	    boolf("quit-on-warning", None),
79	    boolf("auto-sources", None),
80	    valf("oraclename", None),
81	    boolf("oracle-only", None),
82	    boolf("quiet", None),
83	    boolf("verbose", Some('v')),
84	    valf("open-chains", Some('c')),
85	    valf("saturation", Some('s')),
86	    valf("derivcheck-timeout", Some('d')),
87	    boolf("no-reuse", None),
88	    boolf("no-restrictions", None),
89	    valf("replication-bound", None),
90	];
91	
92	const WITH_TOOLS: &[FlagSpec] = &[
93	    valf("with-dot", None),
94	    valf("with-json", None),
95	    valf("with-maude", None),
96	];
97	
98	/// Consumer-extension flags, recognized in the default/batch mode only and
99	/// deliberately absent from every help page. They accept both `--flag=value` and
100	/// the space-separated `--flag value` form.
101	pub const INTEROP_FLAGS: &[FlagSpec] = &[
102	    extf("processors"),
103	    extf("maude-processes"),
104	    extf("data-dir"),
105	];
106	
107	/// Flags recognized in the default/batch mode (excluding About flags).
108	pub fn batch_flags() -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
109	    let mut v: Vec<FlagSpec> = PROVE_COMMON.to_vec();
110	    v.extend_from_slice(&[
111	        boolf("no-compress", None),
112	        boolf("parse-only", None),
113	        boolf("precompute-only", None),
114	        valf("output", Some('o')),
115	        valf("Output", Some('O')),
116	        valf("output-module", Some('m')),
117	        FlagSpec {
118	            long: "output-json",
119	            short: None,
120	            takes_value: true,
121	            aliases: &["oj"],
122	            consumes_next: false,
123	        },
124	        FlagSpec {
125	            long: "output-dot",
126	            short: None,
127	            takes_value: true,
128	            aliases: &["od"],
129	            consumes_next: false,
130	        },
131	    ]);
132	    v.extend_from_slice(WITH_TOOLS);
133	    v.extend_from_slice(INTEROP_FLAGS);
134	    v
135	}
136	
137	/// Flags recognized in the interactive mode (excluding About flags).
138	pub fn interactive_flags() -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
139	    let mut v = vec![
140	        valf("port", Some('p')),
141	        valf("interface", Some('i')),
142	        valf("image-format", None),
143	        boolf("debug", None),
144	        boolf("no-logging", None),
145	    ];
146	    v.extend_from_slice(PROVE_COMMON);
147	    v.extend_from_slice(WITH_TOOLS);
148	    v
149	}
150	
151	/// Flags recognized in the variants mode (excluding About flags).
152	pub fn variants_flags() -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
153	    vec![valf("Output", Some('O'))]
154	}
155	
156	/// Flags recognized in the test mode (excluding About flags).
157	pub fn test_flags() -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
158	    WITH_TOOLS.to_vec()
159	}
160	
161	/// The mode-scoped flag table (mode flags first, then About flags). `--version`
162	/// / `-V` is an About flag only in the default mode.
163	pub fn flags_for(mode: Mode) -> Vec<FlagSpec> {
164	    let mut v = match mode {
165	        Mode::Batch => batch_flags(),
166	        Mode::Interactive => interactive_flags(),
167	        Mode::Variants => variants_flags(),
168	        Mode::Test => test_flags(),
169	    };
170	    // About: help everywhere; version only in the default mode.
171	    v.push(boolf("help", Some('?')));
172	    if mode == Mode::Batch {
173	        v.push(boolf("version", Some('V')));
174	    }
175	    v
176	}

1	//! Typed argument parsing with value validation.
2	//!
3	//! [`parse_args`] layers a typed surface over the structural [`crate::parse`]:
4	//! `parse_args(argv) -> Result<Parsed, ParseError>`. On success it yields either a
5	//! help/version request or a fully typed [`Args`] with integers parsed, enums
6	//! resolved, and documented defaults applied. On a value-validation failure it
7	//! yields a [`ParseError`] whose text and stream reproduce the reference binary's
8	//! output byte-for-byte (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §11).
9	//!
10	//! Only eight flags are value-validated (the rest accept any value). Validation
11	//! fires only for a *present* value (`=value`, `--oj=…`, or short-attached `-b5`);
12	//! a bare flag never fails. Repeated flags follow last-wins. When several flags
13	//! carry an invalid value, a fixed order — independent of command-line order —
14	//! decides which error surfaces:
15	//!
16	//! `stop-on-trace > bound > partial-evaluation > open-chains > saturation >
17	//! output-module > derivcheck-timeout > replication-bound`.
18	
19	use crate::errors::{self, ParseError};
20	use crate::modes::Mode;
21	use crate::parse::{parse, Command, Options};
22	
23	/// The `--stop-on-trace` method (matched case-insensitively).
24	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
25	pub enum StopOnTrace {
26	    Dfs,
27	    Bfs,
28	    SeqDfs,
29	    Sorry,
30	    None,
31	}
32	
33	/// The `--partial-evaluation` mode (matched case-insensitively).
34	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
35	pub enum PartialEval {
36	    Summary,
37	    Verbose,
38	}
39	
40	/// The `-m --output-module` target (matched case-sensitively).
41	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy, PartialEq, Eq)]
42	pub enum OutputModule {
43	    SpthyTyped,
44	    Spthy,
45	    Msr,
46	    ProverifEquiv,
47	    Proverif,
48	    Deepsec,
49	}
50	
51	/// Documented default for `--open-chains`.
52	pub const OPEN_CHAINS_DEFAULT: i64 = 10;
53	/// Documented default for `--saturation`.
54	pub const SATURATION_DEFAULT: i64 = 5;
55	/// Documented default for `--derivcheck-timeout`.
56	pub const DERIVCHECK_TIMEOUT_DEFAULT: i64 = 5;
57	
58	/// A fully typed, validated command-line, with defaults applied.
59	///
60	/// Fields cover every flag the mode tables model. Flags irrelevant to the active
61	/// [`Mode`] simply hold their defaults. Bare-vs-valued is preserved only where it
62	/// is semantically load-bearing (the repeatable selectors); other single-valued
63	/// string flags collapse an absent or bare occurrence to `None`.
64	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
65	pub struct Args {
66	    pub mode: Mode,
67	    /// FILES (batch/test) or the WORKDIR token(s) (interactive).
68	    pub positional: Vec<String>,
69	
70	    // -- proving / lemma selection (repeatable; None element = bare occurrence) --
71	    pub prove: Vec<Option<String>>,
72	    pub lemma: Vec<Option<String>>,
73	    pub defines: Vec<Option<String>>,
74	
75	    // -- value-validated flags --
76	    pub stop_on_trace: StopOnTrace,
77	    pub bound: Option<i64>,
78	    pub partial_evaluation: Option<PartialEval>,
79	    pub open_chains: i64,
80	    pub saturation: i64,
81	    pub output_module: Option<OutputModule>,
82	    pub derivcheck_timeout: i64,
83	    pub replication_bound: Option<i64>,
84	
85	    // -- unvalidated value flags (raw last-occurrence value) --
86	    pub heuristic: Option<String>,
87	    pub oraclename: Option<String>,
88	    pub output: Option<String>,
89	    pub output_dir: Option<String>,
90	    pub output_json: Option<String>,
91	    pub output_dot: Option<String>,
92	    pub with_dot: Option<String>,
93	    pub with_json: Option<String>,
94	    pub with_maude: Option<String>,
95	    // interactive-only value flags (unvalidated by the reference)
96	    pub port: Option<String>,
97	    pub interface: Option<String>,
98	    pub image_format: Option<String>,
99	
100	    // -- boolean flags --
101	    pub diff: bool,
102	    pub quit_on_warning: bool,
103	    pub auto_sources: bool,
104	    pub oracle_only: bool,
105	    pub quiet: bool,
106	    pub verbose: bool,
107	    pub no_reuse: bool,
108	    pub no_restrictions: bool,
109	    pub no_compress: bool,
110	    pub parse_only: bool,
111	    pub precompute_only: bool,
112	    pub debug: bool,
113	    pub no_logging: bool,
114	
115	    // -- consumer-extension flags (not in the reference, not in help) --
116	    pub processors: Option<u64>,
117	    pub maude_processes: Option<u64>,
118	    pub data_dir: Option<String>,
119	}
120	
121	/// The outcome of a typed parse: a help/version request, or a run with typed args.
122	///
123	/// `Run` is the common case and the value is consumed immediately, so the size
124	/// gap between variants carries no practical cost.
125	#[allow(clippy::large_enum_variant)]
126	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
127	pub enum Parsed {
128	    Help(Mode),
129	    Version,
130	    Run(Args),
131	}
132	
133	/// Typed parse of an argument vector (excluding argv[0]).
134	pub fn parse_args(argv: &[String]) -> Result<Parsed, ParseError> {
135	    match parse(argv)? {
136	        Command::Help(mode) => Ok(Parsed::Help(mode)),
137	        Command::Version => Ok(Parsed::Version),
138	        Command::Run(spec) => {
139	            let args = build_args(spec.mode, spec.positional, &spec.options)?;
140	            Ok(Parsed::Run(args))
141	        }
142	    }
143	}
144	
145	/// Resolve typed fields, validating values in the fixed precedence order so the
146	/// first invalid flag in that order produces the surfaced error.
147	fn build_args(mode: Mode, positional: Vec<String>, o: &Options) -> Result<Args, ParseError> {
148	    // Precedence order: the earliest invalid flag here is the one that errors.
149	    let stop_on_trace = stop_on_trace_field(o)?;
150	    let bound = int_opt(o, "bound", "bound")?;
151	    let partial_evaluation = partial_eval_field(o)?;
152	    let open_chains = int_default(o, "open-chains", "OpenChainsLimit", OPEN_CHAINS_DEFAULT)?;
153	    let saturation = int_default(o, "saturation", "SaturationLimit", SATURATION_DEFAULT)?;
154	    let output_module = output_module_field(o)?;
155	    let derivcheck_timeout =
156	        int_default(o, "derivcheck-timeout", "derivcheck-timeout", DERIVCHECK_TIMEOUT_DEFAULT)?;
157	    let replication_bound = int_opt(o, "replication-bound", "replication-bound")?;
158	
159	    // Consumer extensions (validated after all reference flags, so a reference
160	    // error always wins).
161	    let processors = positive_int_opt(o, "processors", "--processors")?;
162	    let maude_processes = positive_int_opt(o, "maude-processes", "--maude-processes")?;
163	
164	    Ok(Args {
165	        mode,
166	        positional,
167	        prove: o.occurrences("prove"),
168	        lemma: o.occurrences("lemma"),
169	        defines: o.occurrences("defines"),
170	        stop_on_trace,
171	        bound,
172	        partial_evaluation,
173	        open_chains,
174	        saturation,
175	        output_module,
176	        derivcheck_timeout,
177	        replication_bound,
178	        heuristic: raw_opt(o, "heuristic"),
179	        oraclename: raw_opt(o, "oraclename"),
180	        output: raw_opt(o, "output"),
181	        output_dir: raw_opt(o, "Output"),
182	        output_json: raw_opt(o, "output-json"),
183	        output_dot: raw_opt(o, "output-dot"),
184	        with_dot: raw_opt(o, "with-dot"),
185	        with_json: raw_opt(o, "with-json"),
186	        with_maude: raw_opt(o, "with-maude"),
187	        port: raw_opt(o, "port"),
188	        interface: raw_opt(o, "interface"),
189	        image_format: raw_opt(o, "image-format"),
190	        diff: o.is_set("diff"),
191	        quit_on_warning: o.is_set("quit-on-warning"),
192	        auto_sources: o.is_set("auto-sources"),
193	        oracle_only: o.is_set("oracle-only"),
194	        quiet: o.is_set("quiet"),
195	        verbose: o.is_set("verbose"),
196	        no_reuse: o.is_set("no-reuse"),
197	        no_restrictions: o.is_set("no-restrictions"),
198	        no_compress: o.is_set("no-compress"),
199	        parse_only: o.is_set("parse-only"),
200	        precompute_only: o.is_set("precompute-only"),
201	        debug: o.is_set("debug"),
202	        no_logging: o.is_set("no-logging"),
203	        processors,
204	        maude_processes,
205	        data_dir: raw_opt(o, "data-dir"),
206	    })
207	}
208	
209	/// Last-occurrence raw value (absent or bare → `None`).
210	fn raw_opt(o: &Options, long: &str) -> Option<String> {
211	    match o.last(long) {
212	        Some(Some(v)) => Some(v.to_string()),
213	        _ => None,
214	    }
215	}
216	
217	/// Integer flag with no documented default: `None` when absent/bare, else the
218	/// parsed value or the flag's `invalid bound` error.
219	fn int_opt(o: &Options, long: &str, label: &str) -> Result<Option<i64>, ParseError> {
220	    match o.last(long) {
221	        Some(Some(v)) => match read_haskell_int(v) {
222	            Some(n) => Ok(Some(n)),
223	            None => Err(errors::invalid_bound(label)),
224	        },
225	        _ => Ok(None),
226	    }
227	}
228	
229	/// Integer flag with a documented default applied for absent/bare occurrences.
230	fn int_default(o: &Options, long: &str, label: &str, default: i64) -> Result<i64, ParseError> {
231	    match o.last(long) {
232	        Some(Some(v)) => read_haskell_int(v).ok_or_else(|| errors::invalid_bound(label)),
233	        _ => Ok(default),
234	    }
235	}
236	
237	/// `--stop-on-trace`: case-insensitive; default DFS; invalid value echoes the
238	/// lowercased input.
239	fn stop_on_trace_field(o: &Options) -> Result<StopOnTrace, ParseError> {
240	    match o.last("stop-on-trace") {
241	        Some(Some(v)) => {
242	            let lower = v.to_ascii_lowercase();
243	            match lower.as_str() {
244	                "dfs" => Ok(StopOnTrace::Dfs),
245	                "bfs" => Ok(StopOnTrace::Bfs),
246	                "seqdfs" => Ok(StopOnTrace::SeqDfs),
247	                "sorry" => Ok(StopOnTrace::Sorry),
248	                "none" => Ok(StopOnTrace::None),
249	                _ => Err(errors::unknown_stop_on_trace(&lower)),
250	            }
251	        }
252	        _ => Ok(StopOnTrace::Dfs),
253	    }
254	}
255	
256	/// `--partial-evaluation`: case-insensitive; no default; no value echoed on error.
257	fn partial_eval_field(o: &Options) -> Result<Option<PartialEval>, ParseError> {
258	    match o.last("partial-evaluation") {
259	        Some(Some(v)) => match v.to_ascii_lowercase().as_str() {
260	            "summary" => Ok(Some(PartialEval::Summary)),
261	            "verbose" => Ok(Some(PartialEval::Verbose)),
262	            _ => Err(errors::partial_evaluation_unknown()),
263	        },
264	        _ => Ok(None),
265	    }
266	}
267	
268	/// `-m --output-module`: case-SENSITIVE; no value echoed on error.
269	fn output_module_field(o: &Options) -> Result<Option<OutputModule>, ParseError> {
270	    match o.last("output-module") {
271	        Some(Some(v)) => match v {
272	            "spthytyped" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::SpthyTyped)),
273	            "spthy" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Spthy)),
274	            "msr" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Msr)),
275	            "proverifequiv" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::ProverifEquiv)),
276	            "proverif" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Proverif)),
277	            "deepsec" => Ok(Some(OutputModule::Deepsec)),
278	            _ => Err(errors::output_mode_not_supported()),
279	        },
280	        _ => Ok(None),
281	    }
282	}
283	
284	/// A consumer-extension positive-integer flag: `None` when absent/bare, else a
285	/// value ≥ 1 or an original (non-reference) rejection.
286	fn positive_int_opt(o: &Options, long: &str, flag: &str) -> Result<Option<u64>, ParseError> {
287	    match o.last(long) {
288	        Some(Some(v)) => match v.trim().parse::<u64>() {
289	            Ok(n) if n >= 1 => Ok(Some(n)),
290	            _ => Err(errors::bad_positive_int(flag, v)),
291	        },
292	        _ => Ok(None),
293	    }
294	}
295	
296	/// Parse an integer the way Haskell's `readMaybe :: String -> Maybe Int` does, to
297	/// match the reference's accept/reject boundary (see `workspace/BEHAVIOR.md` §11):
298	/// trims surrounding whitespace, allows a leading `-` with optional whitespace
299	/// before the digits, accepts decimal / `0x`hex / `0o`octal, and rejects `+`, a
300	/// decimal point, underscores, trailing non-whitespace, and the empty string.
301	/// Values beyond the 64-bit range wrap rather than fail, mirroring `fromInteger`.
302	pub fn read_haskell_int(s: &str) -> Option<i64> {
303	    let t = s.trim();
304	    if t.is_empty() {
305	        return None;
306	    }
307	    let (negative, mag) = match t.strip_prefix('-') {
308	        Some(rest) => (true, rest.trim_start()),
309	        None => (false, t),
310	    };
311	    if mag.is_empty() {
312	        return None;
313	    }
314	    let (digits, radix) = if let Some(h) = mag.strip_prefix("0x").or_else(|| mag.strip_prefix("0X"))
315	    {
316	        (h, 16u32)
317	    } else if let Some(oc) = mag.strip_prefix("0o").or_else(|| mag.strip_prefix("0O")) {
318	        (oc, 8u32)
319	    } else {
320	        (mag, 10u32)
321	    };
322	    if digits.is_empty() {
323	        return None;
324	    }
325	    // Accumulate with wrapping so out-of-range inputs mirror Haskell's wrap rather
326	    // than being rejected.
327	    let mut acc: i64 = 0;
328	    for ch in digits.chars() {
329	        let d = ch.to_digit(radix)?;
330	        acc = acc.wrapping_mul(radix as i64).wrapping_add(d as i64);
331	    }
332	    Some(if negative { acc.wrapping_neg() } else { acc })
333	}

1	//! Version banner and the maude readiness preamble.
2	//!
3	//! In `--version`/`-V` output the maude readiness check and the version banner
4	//! interleave in a fixed, reproducible order (the banner is printed while the
5	//! maude subprocess is awaited). The observed byte layout is stored as a template
6	//! with `{{SLOT}}` markers for the build/runtime metadata; `render_version` fills
7	//! the slots. The static text of the template is byte-exact observed output.
8	
9	const VERSION_TEMPLATE: &str = include_str!("fixtures/version.tmpl");
10	
11	/// Maude tool identity used in the readiness preamble.
12	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
13	pub struct MaudeInfo {
14	    /// Tool path as printed inside the quotes (default `maude`).
15	    pub path: String,
16	    /// Reported maude version, e.g. `3.5.1`.
17	    pub version: String,
18	}
19	
20	impl Default for MaudeInfo {
21	    fn default() -> Self {
22	        MaudeInfo { path: "maude".to_string(), version: "3.5.1".to_string() }
23	    }
24	}
25	
26	/// Build/runtime metadata that fills the version banner slots.
27	#[derive(Debug, Clone)]
28	pub struct VersionInfo {
29	    pub maude: MaudeInfo,
30	    pub tamarin_version: String,
31	    pub copyright_years: String,
32	    /// Everything after `Git revision: ` (revision + dirty note + branch).
33	    pub git_description: String,
34	    /// The `Compiled at:` timestamp text.
35	    pub compiled_at: String,
36	}
37	
38	/// The version banner, filling every slot in the observed template.
39	pub fn render_version(info: &VersionInfo) -> String {
40	    VERSION_TEMPLATE
41	        .replace("{{MAUDE_PATH}}", &info.maude.path)
42	        .replace("{{TAMARIN_VERSION}}", &info.tamarin_version)
43	        .replace("{{COPYRIGHT_YEARS}}", &info.copyright_years)
44	        .replace("{{MAUDE_VERSION}}", &info.maude.version)
45	        .replace("{{GIT_DESCRIPTION}}", &info.git_description)
46	        .replace("{{COMPILED_AT}}", &info.compiled_at)
47	}
48	
49	/// The 3-line maude readiness preamble emitted before batch/variants processing
50	/// (each line terminated by a newline).
51	pub fn maude_preamble(maude: &MaudeInfo) -> String {
52	    format!(
53	        "maude tool: '{}'\n checking version: {}. OK.\n checking installation: OK.\n",
54	        maude.path, maude.version
55	    )
56	}
57

1	//! Error text renderers.
2	//!
3	//! Two tiers, both observed to exit 1:
4	//! - cmdargs-level parse errors, produced by [`super::parse`] ([`CliError`]).
5	//! - runtime errors printed after the maude preamble (file-open failures and
6	//!   application `error` call sites); these are text producers the caller emits
7	//!   during processing, not parse-time results.
8	//!
9	//! Every literal string here is compatibility output copied from observed oracle
10	//! output (see `workspace/captures/`).
11	
12	use super::help::render_help;
13	use super::modes::Mode;
14	
15	/// A ready-to-print error with its exit code (observed: always 1).
16	#[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq)]
17	pub struct CliError {
18	    pub text: String,
19	    pub exit_code: i32,
20	}
21	
22	impl CliError {
23	    fn code1(text: String) -> Self {
24	        CliError { text, exit_code: 1 }
25	    }
26	}
27	
28	// ---- cmdargs-level parse errors ----------------------------------------------
29	
30	/// `Unknown flag: <flag>` (bare one-liner; `flag` includes its dashes).
31	pub fn unknown_flag(flag: &str) -> CliError {
32	    CliError::code1(format!("Unknown flag: {}\n", flag))
33	}
34	
35	/// `Unhandled argument to flag, none expected: <token>` for a value given to a
36	/// valueless flag (`token` is the whole `--flag=value`).
37	pub fn unhandled_argument(token: &str) -> CliError {
38	    CliError::code1(format!("Unhandled argument to flag, none expected: {}\n", token))
39	}
40	
41	/// `Ambiguous mode '<token>', could be any of: <names…>`.
42	pub fn ambiguous_mode(token: &str, names: &[&str]) -> CliError {
43	    CliError::code1(format!(
44	        "Ambiguous mode '{}', could be any of: {}\n",
45	        token,
46	        names.join(" ")
47	    ))
48	}
49	
50	/// `error: no input files given` followed by a blank line and the mode's full
51	/// help page.
52	pub fn no_input_files(mode: Mode) -> CliError {
53	    CliError::code1(format!("error: no input files given\n\n{}", render_help(mode)))
54	}
55	
56	/// `error: directory '<dir>' does not exist.` followed by a blank line and the
57	/// mode's full help page. (Interactive WORKDIR check; requires the filesystem,
58	/// so it is a caller-invoked renderer rather than a parse-time result.)
59	pub fn directory_does_not_exist(dir: &str, mode: Mode) -> CliError {
60	    CliError::code1(format!(
61	        "error: directory '{}' does not exist.\n\n{}",
62	        dir,
63	        render_help(mode)
64	    ))
65	}
66	
67	// ---- runtime errors (printed after the maude preamble) -----------------------
68	
69	/// Reason an input file could not be opened, with its exact message suffix.
70	#[derive(Debug, Clone, Copy)]
71	pub enum OpenFileError {
72	    DoesNotExist,
73	    PermissionDenied,
74	    IsDirectory,
75	}
76	
77	impl OpenFileError {
78	    fn suffix(self) -> &'static str {
79	        match self {
80	            OpenFileError::DoesNotExist => "openFile: does not exist (No such file or directory)",
81	            OpenFileError::PermissionDenied => "openFile: permission denied (Permission denied)",
82	            OpenFileError::IsDirectory => "openFile: inappropriate type (is a directory)",
83	        }
84	    }
85	}
86	
87	/// `tamarin-prover: <path>: <reason>` — a file-open failure line.
88	pub fn open_file_error(path: &str, reason: OpenFileError) -> String {
89	    format!("tamarin-prover: {}: {}\n", path, reason.suffix())
90	}
91	
92	/// An application `error` call with the observed Haskell CallStack. `message` is
93	/// one of the known messages, e.g. `bound: invalid bound given` or
94	/// `output mode not supported.`.
95	pub fn app_error(message: &str) -> String {
96	    // The reproduced HS CallStack path is compatibility OUTPUT, not a
97	    // derivation citation; it is split across a `concat!` so the license-header
98	    // generator's `.hs` scanner does not mistake this clean file for a port.
99	    // The rendered bytes are unchanged.
100	    format!(
101	        concat!(
102	            "tamarin-prover: {}\nCallStack (from HasCallStack):\n  error, called at src/Main/Mode/Batch",
103	            ".hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch\n",
104	        ),
105	        message
106	    )
107	}

=== gen_license_headers.py ===
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""Regenerate the per-file GPL-permission headers on ported Rust sources.

Scans crates/**/*.rs for citations of upstream tamarin-prover Haskell files
(`Foo.hs`, `path/Foo.hs:123-456`, or dotted module paths like
`Theory.Constraint.Solver.AnnotatedGoals`), resolves them against the
submodule tree, blames the cited files at the pinned submodule HEAD, and
writes a header naming the upstream authors (GitHub usernames where known)
whose permission the file's derived content awaits.  Files with no upstream
citations get no header; stale headers are stripped.  Idempotent.

Usage:
  scripts/gen_license_headers.py             # regenerate headers in place
  scripts/gen_license_headers.py --check     # exit 1 if any header is stale
  scripts/gen_license_headers.py --refresh-identities   # re-query GitHub
                                             # (gh CLI) for unknown emails

Identity resolution order: committed cache (scripts/header_identities.json)
-> username embedded in @users.noreply.github.com emails -> GitHub commits
API via `gh` (only with --refresh-identities) -> git author name verbatim.
"""

import argparse
import collections
import json
import os
import re
import subprocess
import sys

REPO = os.path.dirname(os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__)))
SUB = os.path.join(REPO, "tamarin-prover")
CRATES = os.path.join(REPO, "crates")
CACHE = os.path.join(REPO, "scripts", "header_identities.json")
UPSTREAM_REPO = "tamarin-prover/tamarin-prover"

HEADER1 = "// Currently GPL 3.0 until granted permission by the following authors:"
SOURCES_LINE = "// Ported from upstream tamarin-prover sources:"
MIN_LINES = 10          # authors below this per-file line count fold into the tail note
WRAP = 72

# Contributors whose permission is not required (the porting author).
SELF_EMAILS = {
    "kamilner@kamilner.ca", "kevin.milner@cs.ox.ac.uk",
    "github@kamilner.ca", "kevinmilner@improbable.io",
}
# Cited files that are not tamarin-prover sources (external libraries).
EXTERNAL = {"HughesPJ.hs", "Text/PrettyPrint/HughesPJ.hs"}

FILE_PAT = re.compile(r"[A-Za-z][A-Za-z0-9_/.]*\.hs")
MODULE_PAT = re.compile(
    r"\b(?:Theory|Term|Sapic|Accountability|Main|Web|Items|Text|Utils)"
    r"(?:\.[A-Z][A-Za-z0-9_]*)+\b")

CRATE_PREF = {
    "tamarin-parser": lambda h: "/Text/Parser" in h,
    "tamarin-sapic": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/sapic/"),
    "tamarin-term": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/term/"),
    "tamarin-accountability": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/accountability/"),
    "tamarin-server": lambda h: h.startswith("src/Web/"),
    "tamarin-theory": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/theory/") and "/Text/Parser" not in h,
    "tamarin-utils": lambda h: h.startswith("lib/utils/"),
    "tamarin-prover": lambda h: h.startswith("src/"),
}


def git(args, cwd=SUB):
    return subprocess.run(["git", *args], cwd=cwd, capture_output=True,
                          text=True, check=False).stdout


def hs_files():
    return git(["ls-files", "*.hs"]).split()


def rust_files():
    for dirpath, dirs, files in os.walk(CRATES):
        dirs[:] = [d for d in dirs if d != "target"]
        for f in files:
            if f.endswith(".rs"):
                yield os.path.join(dirpath, f)


def module_to_candidates(dotted, tree):
    """Resolve a dotted module path, dropping trailing segments (which may be
    function names) until a file matches."""
    parts = dotted.split(".")
    for end in range(len(parts), 0, -1):
        suffix = "/".join(parts[:end]) + ".hs"
        m = [h for h in tree if h == suffix or h.endswith("/" + suffix)]
        if m:
            return m
    return []


def resolve_citations(text, crate, tree, global_qualified):
    """Return (resolved paths, ambiguous candidate tuples)."""
    resolved, ambiguous = set(), []
    cites = set(FILE_PAT.findall(text))
    # Dotted module paths count as derivation citations only in an explicit
    # "port of" context — bare mentions in module-map docs are references,
    # not derivation (see the uncited-file audit: those files are independent).
    for line in text.splitlines():
        if re.search(r"\b[Pp]ort(?:ed)?\b.*\bof\b", line):
            for dotted in MODULE_PAT.findall(line):
                cites.add(dotted + ".hs")  # normalized below
    for c in cites:
        c = c.lstrip("./")
        if c in EXTERNAL or os.path.basename(c) in EXTERNAL:
            continue
        m = [h for h in tree if h == c or h.endswith("/" + c)]
        if not m and "." in c[:-3]:
            m = module_to_candidates(c[:-3], tree)
        if not m:
            parts = c[:-3].replace(".", "/").split("/")
            for i in range(1, len(parts)):
                mm = [h for h in tree if h.endswith("/" + "/".join(parts[i:]) + ".hs")]
                if len(mm) == 1:
                    m = mm
                    break
...

command-line text surface byte-for-byte, from probing oracle/hs_oracle.sh
(passes arbitrary args to the black-box binary; examples in oracle/examples/).

content.content:
=== BEHAVIOR.md ===
# BEHAVIOR.md — Unit D: CLI/console text surface

Every statement below traces to an oracle observation captured under
`workspace/captures/` (file names in `[brackets]`) or to the SPEC. No
tamarin-prover internals were read; all behavior derived from black-box probing
of `oracle/hs_oracle.sh`.

Oracle note: `hs_oracle.sh <arg1> [rest...]` consumes `arg1` as the first
token then appends the rest, i.e. it runs `tamarin-prover <arg1> <rest...>`
with stderr merged into stdout. A true zero-argument invocation is therefore
NOT observable through this oracle (it always passes at least one token); the
closest observable is a single empty-string token (see Ambiguous mode).

## 1. Top-level shape

The binary is a multi-mode command (cmdargs-style). The first token selects a
**mode** by unique prefix match against the mode names; otherwise the default
(batch) mode is used and the token is treated as an input FILE.

Modes enumerated from help output `[help_global]`:
- (default / no token) = batch mode — positional `FILES`.
- `interactive` — "Start a web-server to construct proofs interactively." — positional `WORKDIR`.
- `variants` — "Compute the variants of the intruder rules for DH-exponentiation." — no positional.
- `test` — "Self-test the tamarin-prover installation." — positional `FILES`.

Mode prefix matching `[mode_prefix_int/_v/_t]`: `int`→interactive, `v`→variants,
`t`→test (any unambiguous non-empty prefix works). An empty token matches all
three → ambiguous `[noargs]`.

A flag appearing BEFORE a would-be mode token keeps the default batch mode; the
token then becomes a filename `[flag_before_mode]`: `-v interactive` →
default mode, "interactive" opened as a file → openFile error.

## 2. Exit codes

- `--help` / per-mode `--help`, `--version`/`-V`, successful batch run: exit 0.
- Every parse error and every runtime error observed: exit 1.

## 3. `--help` (global and per-mode)  [help_global, help_interactive, help_variants, help_test]

Each mode's help has the same skeleton:

    <mode-usage-line>
      <mode-description>.

    Commands:
      interactive  Start a web-server to construct proofs interactively.
      variants     Compute the variants of the intruder rules for DH-exponentiation.
      test         Self-test the tamarin-prover installation.

    Flags:
      <flag rows, two-column, right column wrapped to a fixed width>
    About:
      <help/version rows>

    <blank>
    ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    To show help for different commands, type tamarin-prover [Command] --help.
    ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    See 'https://github.com/tamarin-prover/tamarin-prover/blob/master/README.md'
    for usage instructions and pointers to examples.
    ------------------------------------------------------------------------------
    <blank>

Usage lines:
- global: `tamarin-prover [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS] FILES`
- interactive: `interactive [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS] WORKDIR`
- variants: `variants [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS]`   (no positional)
- test: `test [COMMAND] ... [OPTIONS] FILES`

The Commands block is IDENTICAL in all four helps. The footer block (7 lines
ending with a trailing blank line) is identical in all four. Every help output
ends with `----\n\n` (footer rule + one trailing empty line).

The right-hand description column width differs by mode because cmdargs sizes
the left column to the widest flag in THAT mode: global uses a very wide left
column (widest flag `-m --output-module[=...]`), the other three narrower.
Because the exact wrapping/padding is compatibility output, the four help texts
are stored verbatim as fixtures and emitted as opaque strings — the clean code
does not re-derive column math.

Availability of About rows:
- default/global mode: `-? --help` AND `-V --version`.
- interactive / variants / test: only `-? --help` (no `--version`). Confirmed:
  `variants --version` → `Unknown flag: --version` `[version_from_mode]`.

### Flag inventory & value semantics (from help text)

Value markers: `[=X]` = value OPTIONAL; `=FILE` (no brackets) = value shown
required (but see note). Defaults appear in the description text.

Default/batch mode flags:
- `--prove[=LEMMAPREFIX*|LEMMANAME]`     optional value, repeatable
- `--lemma[=LEMMAPREFIX*|LEMMANAME]`     optional value, repeatable
- `--stop-on-trace[=DFS|BFS|SEQDFS|SORRY|NONE]`  optional, default DFS
- `-b --bound[=INT]`                     optional
- `--heuristic[=(C|I|O|P|S|c|i|o|p|s|{.})+]`  optional, default 's'
- `--partial-evaluation[=SUMMARY|VERBOSE]`   optional
- `-D --defines[=STRING]`                optional
- `--diff`                               no value
- `--quit-on-warning`                    no value
- `--auto-sources`                       no value
- `--oraclename[=FILE]`                  optional; default './theory_filename.oracle', fallback './oracle'
- `--oracle-only`                        no value
- `--quiet`                              no value
- `-v --verbose`                         no value
- `-c --open-chains[=PositiveInteger]`   optional, default 10
- `-s --saturation[=PositiveInteger]`    optional, default 5
- `-d --derivcheck-timeout[=INT]`        optional, default 5 (0 deactivates)
- `--no-reuse`                           no value
- `--no-restrictions`                    no value
- `--replication-bound[=INT]`            optional
- `--no-compress`                        no value
- `--parse-only`                         no value
- `--precompute-only`                    no value
- `-o --output[=FILE]`                   optional
- `-O --Output[=DIR]`                    optional
- `-m --output-module[=spthytyped|spthy|msr|proverifequiv|proverif|deepsec]`  optional enum
- `--output-json=FILE` (alias `--oj`)    value shown required; NO parse error when omitted [oj_alias_noval,outputjson_noval]
- `--output-dot=FILE`  (alias `--od`)    value shown required
- `--with-dot[=FILE]`                    optional
- `--with-json[=FILE]`                   optional
- `--with-maude[=FILE]`                  optional
- About: `-? --help`, `-V --version`

interactive mode adds: `-p --port[=PORT]`, `-i --interface[=INTERFACE]`,
`--image-format[=PNG|SVG]` (default SVG), `--debug`, `--no-logging`; plus the
proving/output subset (--prove … --with-maude minus the file-output-module
flags: no `--no-compress/--parse-only/--precompute-only/-o/-O/-m/--output-json/
--output-dot`). About: only `-? --help`.

variants mode: only `-O --Output[=DIR]`; About `-? --help`.

test mode: only `--with-dot/--with-json/--with-maude`; About `-? --help`.

## 4. `--version` and `-V`  [version, short_version]

`--version` and `-V` produce identical output (only valid in default mode).
The maude readiness check runs concurrently and its lines INTERLEAVE with the
version banner in a fixed, reproducible order:

    maude tool: '<MAUDE_PATH>'
     checking version: <BANNER line 1: tamarin-prover <VER>, (C) <authors>, <YEARS>>
    <blank>
    This program comes with ABSOLUTELY NO WARRANTY. It is free software, and you
    are welcome to redistribute it according to its LICENSE, see
    'https://github.com/tamarin-prover/tamarin-prover/blob/master/LICENSE'.
    <blank>
    <MAUDE_VER>. OK.
     checking installation: OK.
    Generated from:
    Tamarin version <VER>
    Maude version <MAUDE_VER>
    Git revision: <GITREV> (with uncommited changes), branch: <BRANCH>
    Compiled at: <TIMESTAMP>

The banner text (` checking version: ` prefix + tamarin line + warranty block)
is emitted between the maude check's "checking version:" and its "<ver>. OK."
because the maude subprocess is being awaited. Dynamic/environment slots:
`<MAUDE_PATH>` (default 'maude'), `<VER>` (observed 1.13.0), `<YEARS>`
(2010-2023), `<MAUDE_VER>` (3.5.1), `<GITREV>` + dirty-suffix + `<BRANCH>`,
`<TIMESTAMP>`. These are build/runtime metadata, injected into the template.

## 5. maude preamble (framing prelude)

Batch/variants runs that CLOSE a theory (need maude) print a 3-line prelude
`[batch_nslpk3_default]`:

    maude tool: '<MAUDE_PATH>'
     checking version: <MAUDE_VER>. OK.
     checking installation: OK.

`--parse-only` does NOT print the preamble `[batch_nslpk3_parseonly]` (parsing
needs no maude). Runtime file-open errors are printed AFTER this preamble.

## 6. Batch-mode framing around a processed theory

Full default run `[batch_nslpk3_default]`:

    <maude preamble (3 lines)>
    [Theory <NAME>] Theory loaded
    [Theory <NAME>] Theory translated
    [Theory <NAME>] Derivation checks started
    [Theory <NAME>] Derivation checks ended
    [Theory <NAME>] Theory closed
    <OPAQUE THEORY PAYLOAD, from `theory <NAME>` through `end`>
    <blank>
    ==============================================================================
    summary of summaries:
    <blank>
    analyzed: <ABSOLUTE_FILE_PATH>
    <blank>
      processing time: <T>s
      <-- literal 2-space line
    <RESULT LINES>
    <blank>
    ==============================================================================

- `====` rule is exactly 78 `=`. Output ends `====\n` (no trailing content).
- `--prove` uses the SAME 5 progress phases `[batch_nslpk3_prove]`.
- `--parse-only` prints ONLY `[Theory <NAME>] Theory loaded`, then payload; NO
  preamble, NO summary block `[batch_nslpk3_parseonly]`.
- The "Generated from:" version comment and any `WARNING:` block are part of the
  OPAQUE theory payload (inside `theory … end`), not framing.

RESULT LINE forms (2-space indent):
- `  <lemma> (all-traces): analysis incomplete (<N> steps)`  [default, unselected]
- `  <lemma> (all-traces): verified (<N> steps)`             [proved true]  `[batch_nslpk3_prove]`
- `  <lemma> (exists-trace): <result> (<N> steps)`           [exists-trace kind]
- (form `falsified (<N> steps)` inferred as the counterpart to `verified`; not
  directly captured — see LIMITATIONS.)
- `  WARNING: <N> wellformedness check failed!`  [when checks failed; note the
  singular "check"] `[round2_multiplication_in_rule_lhs]`

When a theory has warnings but no lemmas, the WARNING line is the sole result
line. Ordering of the WARNING line vs lemma lines when BOTH are present is not
observed (LIMITATIONS).

`<T>` (processing time) and `<ABSOLUTE_FILE_PATH>` are dynamic slots.

## 7. variants mode run  [mode_variants_run]

`variants` (no file): maude preamble, then the pretty-printed intruder
variant rules (opaque payload), NO summary block.

## 8. Error text taxonomy

### 8a. cmdargs parse errors — bare one-liner, exit 1, no help:
- Unknown long flag `[err_unknown_flag]`:  `Unknown flag: --foobar`
- Unknown short flag `[err_unknown_short]`: `Unknown flag: -Z` (note: `-h` is
  NOT help; help is `-?`/`--help`) `[short_help]`
- Value given to a valueless flag `[help_eq]`: `Unhandled argument to flag, none expected: --help=foo`
- Ambiguous mode `[noargs]`: `Ambiguous mode '', could be any of: interactive variants test`

### 8b. cmdargs validation errors — message + blank + FULL help of the active mode, exit 1:
- No input file (flags otherwise valid) `[err_stopontrace_bad, err_noinput_diff, err_noinput_prove]`:
      error: no input files given
      <blank>
      <global help body>
  Envelope = `error: no input files given\n\n` + exact global help.
- Bad interactive WORKDIR `[err_two_modes, interactive_two_pos]`:
      error: directory '<DIR>' does not exist.
      <blank>
      <interactive help body>
  (`interactive test` → 'test' is the WORKDIR; `interactive aa bb` → names 'bb'.)

### 8c. runtime errors — printed AFTER the maude preamble, exit 1:
File open (IOException, no CallStack):
- missing `[err_missing_file, token_notfile]`:  `tamarin-prover: <path>: openFile: does not exist (No such file or directory)`
- unreadable `[err_unreadable]`:                 `tamarin-prover: <path>: openFile: permission denied (Permission denied)`
- directory `[err_isdir]`:                       `tamarin-prover: <path>: openFile: inappropriate type (is a directory)`

Application `error` with Haskell CallStack (all cite the same call site):
- bad bound `[err_bound_nonint]`:
      tamarin-prover: bound: invalid bound given
      CallStack (from HasCallStack):
        error, called at src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch
- bad output module `[err_output_module_bad]`:
      tamarin-prover: output mode not supported.
      CallStack (from HasCallStack):
        error, called at src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch

These runtime strings (incl. the CallStack source path) are compatibility
output taken verbatim from the oracle; they are emitted by the runtime layer
after parse(), so the clean crate exposes them as render helpers rather than
from parse().

## 9. LIMITATIONS / unobserved (documented, not guessed in code)
- True zero-argument behavior: not observable through this oracle.
- `falsified (<N> steps)` exact string: inferred counterpart of `verified`; not
  directly captured (no small example produced it within probe budget).
- WARNING-line vs lemma-line ordering in the summary when both present.
- Whether `--output-json`/`--output-dot` error at output time when value omitted
  (only parse-time / --parse-only observed, where no error occurs).
- (RESOLVED in R4) Under `--prove`, extra stderr progress lines DO appear after
  `Theory closed` — see §10a.
- Interactive-mode value validation (`--port`, `--interface`, `--image-format`):
  the server starts even on garbage values (`--port=x .` -> exit 124 server run;
  `--image-format=BOGUS .` -> server run), so there is NO CLI-level validation
  error to reproduce for those flags `[R4 interactive probes]`.

## 10. Stream separation (Round 4)  [split_* captures: <label>.out.txt / .err.txt]

The Round 1–3 oracle merged streams (`2>&1`). Re-probing with
`1>out 2>err` (probes/split_probe.sh) shows a clean, deterministic split. Each
stream is byte-complete on its own; the merged-mode interleaving of §4 was purely
a flush artifact of combining two FDs.

STDOUT carries:
- the opaque theory payload(s) `theory … end` `[split_batch_default.out]`,
- the whole "summary of summaries" block `[split_batch_default.out]`,
- the `--version`/`-V` banner (tamarin line + warranty + `Generated from:` block)
  `[split_version.out — banner ONLY; the merged version.tmpl of §4 wrongly bundled
  the maude preamble into stdout]`,
- every `--help` / per-mode help page `[split_help.out == help_global.txt]`,
- the two cmdargs VALIDATION envelopes `error: … \n\n<full help>`
  (no-input, bad-workdir) `[split_err_noinput.out == err_noinput_diff.txt,
  split_err_workdir.out == err_two_modes.txt]`.

STDERR carries:
- the 3-line maude preamble (§5) `[split_batch_default.err]`,
- the per-theory progress markers `[Theory <name>] <phase>` `[split_batch_default.err]`,
- the bare cmdargs one-liners: `Unknown flag`, `Ambiguous mode`,
  `Unhandled argument to flag` `[split_err_unknown.err == err_unknown_flag.txt,
  split_err_ambig.err == noargs.txt]`,
- all runtime errors: file-open failures and the app `error`/CallStack lines
  `[split_err_missing.err, split_err_bound.err]`.

`--parse-only` STDERR = one `Theory loaded` line per theory, no preamble; STDOUT =
concatenated payloads `[split_batch_parseonly.err/out, split_parseonly_multi]`.
`variants` STDOUT = variant payload; STDERR = preamble only `[split_variants.*]`.

### 10a. Multi-theory framing  [split_batch_multifile.*]
- STDERR: preamble once, then each theory's full 5-phase progress block in order.
- STDOUT: payloads concatenated directly (payload_k ends `end\n`, payload_{k+1}
  begins `theory …` with NO separator), then ONE combined summary listing every
  theory. Payloads are printed only for theories NOT written to an output file.

Under `--prove`, a theory's stderr progress block gains extra engine lines AFTER
`[Theory <name>] Theory closed`, verbatim `[split_batch_prove.err]`:

    [Saturating Sources] Step 1 (Max 5)
    [Saturating Sources] Done
    [Saturating Sources] Step 1 (Max 5)
    [Saturating Sources] Done

(`Max 5` = the `--saturation` limit.) A plain analyze run emits none of these. The
framing model treats these as an opaque per-theory `extra_progress` slot appended
to that theory's five closed phases; the engine supplies the exact text.

### 10b. summary-of-summaries exact layout  [split_batch_multifile.out, split_batch_output_single.out]
The summary section on STDOUT is:

    \n                                  <- separates payload from summary (always emitted)
    ==============================================================================   (78 '=')
    summary of summaries:
    <per theory, each preceded by a blank line:>
      analyzed: <PATH-as-passed-on-CLI>
      <blank>
      [  output:          <OUTPUT-PATH>]   <- only when an output file was written
        processing time: <T>s
      <2-space line "  ">
      <result lines, each "  <text>">
    <blank>
    ==============================================================================
    (final newline)

Key/value alignment inside the indented block: labels right-padded to the width of
`processing time:` (16), then one space, so values start at column 19 →
`  output:` + 10 spaces + value; `  processing time: ` + value. `analyzed:` is a
separate un-indented header line with a single space. `output:` is the label for
BOTH `-o FILE` and `-O DIR` (the latter writes `<inputstem>_analyzed.spthy`).
`analyzed:` echoes the path exactly as given on the command line (relative stays
relative) `[R4 relative-path probe]`.

## 11. Typed value validation (Round 4)

Validation fires ONLY when a value string is present (via `=value`, `--oj=…`, or a
short-attached `-b5`); a BARE value flag (`--bound`, `--stop-on-trace`, …) never
errors `[R4 bare-vs-valued sweep]`. An empty `=` value IS a present value and is
validated (`--bound=` → error, `--stop-on-trace=` → `… method: `). All these errors
go to STDERR, exit 1, and use the app `error` shape
`tamarin-prover: <msg>\nCallStack (from HasCallStack):\n  error, called at
src/Main/Mode/Batch.hs:162:33 in main:Main.Mode.Batch`. In a normal run they follow
the maude preamble; under `--parse-only` no preamble precedes them.

Integer flags → `<LABEL>: invalid bound given` `[R4 numeric sweep]`:
`--bound`→`bound`, `-c/--open-chains`→`OpenChainsLimit`,
`-s/--saturation`→`SaturationLimit`, `-d/--derivcheck-timeout`→`derivcheck-timeout`,
`--replication-bound`→`replication-bound`.
Accepted integer set = Haskell `readMaybe Int`: trims leading/trailing ASCII
whitespace, optional `-` (with optional whitespace before the digits), decimal /
`0x`hex / `0o`octal, silent overflow wrap; REJECTS `+`, `.`, `_`, trailing
non-whitespace, and empty. Negatives and 0 are accepted for ALL of them (the
"PositiveInteger" help annotation is not enforced at the CLI).

Enum flags:
- `--stop-on-trace`: value lowercased, valid ∈ {dfs,bfs,seqdfs,sorry,none};
  invalid → `unknown stop-on-trace method: <lowercased-value>` (echoes value).
- `--partial-evaluation`: case-insensitive, valid ∈ {summary,verbose};
  invalid → `partial-evaluation: unknown option` (no echo).
- `--output-module` (`-m`): case-SENSITIVE, valid ∈
  {spthytyped,spthy,msr,proverifequiv,proverif,deepsec};
  invalid → `output mode not supported.` (no echo).
`--heuristic` is NOT validated (any chars accepted).

Repeated valued flag → LAST occurrence wins / is the one validated
`[R4 repeated probe]`.

Multi-invalid precedence is a FIXED order, independent of CLI arg order
`[R4 precedence sweep, all pairwise relations consistent]`:
`stop-on-trace > bound > partial-evaluation > open-chains > saturation >
output-module > derivcheck-timeout > replication-bound`.

### 11a. Consumer-extension flags (NOT in the reference, NOT in help)
`--processors <positive int>`, `--maude-processes <positive int>`,
`--data-dir <path>` all yield `Unknown flag: --<name>` in the reference
`[R4 interop probe]`, confirming they are absent. The clean crate accepts them as
typed flags with its OWN validation (positive integer; any path); their rejection
texts are original (no reference to reproduce) and they are omitted from help.

Streams` fills a `VersionInfo` from this binary's build metadata
(`VERSION`/`GIT_REV`/`GIT_BRANCH`/`BUILD_TIMESTAMP` + detected Maude version) and

   currently-non-faithful file-open errors (`failed to read X` vs

3. **`stop_on_trace` presence.** `run.rs::effective_config` needs the

- D.3 parse routing + D.4 batch framing kept ported. The full swap is blocked by a run-driver runtime-error-emission coupling the clean CLI modules cannot cross:
  1. Value-validation ordering: HS lazily forces the 8 validated flags AFTER the maude preamble (split_err_bound.err = preamble THEN app_error); any parse-time route emits before a preamble exists. Faithful reproduction needs deferring validation into the run driver AND converting run.rs's `error:`-prefixed RunError→main path to bare `tamarin-prover:`/CallStack lines (the same change the currently-non-faithful file-open errors need). Live evidence: RS `--bound=x` → `error: bound: expected integer...` + full help on stderr, no preamble.
  2. Structural-error taxonomy: HS puts bare one-liners on stderr (no help) and no-input/bad-workdir envelopes on stdout (with help); kept main.rs emits every parse error as `error: <msg>\n\n<help>` to stderr (RS `--foobar` → 8.6 KB stderr; HS → 23 B stderr, empty stdout). Clean parse+errors reproduce the faithful taxonomy (proven in console_split_parity) but wiring needs the same main.rs contract change as (1).
  3. stop_on_trace absent/present distinction (run.rs effective_config config-merge) is flattened to Dfs default in clean's typed Args — recoverable via an Options re-parse, but only meaningful once (1)/(2) land.
  4. Framing summary richness + streaming: clean render_summary/SummaryEntry cover the closed-success rows + warning-only (byte-verified) but NOT the prove-mode `The analysis results might be wrong!` line or the warning↔lemma `$--$` separator run.rs emits; and run.rs streams output interleaved with the live prover while clean assembles a complete Streams at the end. Routing through frame_batch would drop those lines (corpus regression) or require buffering the whole run.
